steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Rivera and colleagues hypothesize an inverse relationship between body size and propensity to associate with other species. The waved woodpecker (C. undatus) is large (~58 g) and the trilling gnatwren (R. melanurus) is small (~9 g). A supporting finding must show the large woodpecker associating less with other species and the small gnatwren associating more.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Rivera and colleagues always observed C. undatus alone or in single-species flocks but frequently observed R. melanurus in multispecies flocks.: Large woodpecker stays solo or with its own species; small gnatwren joins multispecies flocks. This is exactly the predicted inverse relationship. ✓
  • Choice B — Rivera and colleagues observed several species approximately the same size as R. melanurus, but only R. melanurus regularly flocked with birds of other species.: Focuses on species-specific behavior among similarly sized birds, not on the body-size relationship the hypothesis predicts. ✗
  • Choice C — Rivera and colleagues occasionally observed R. melanurus flocking with other species but not as frequently as they observed C. undatus doing so.: This reverses the prediction — the large woodpecker joining multispecies flocks more than the small gnatwren contradicts the hypothesis. ✗
  • Choice D — Rivera and colleagues observed that R. melanurus individuals were less likely to be preyed on when in multispecies or single-species flocks than when alone.: Addresses predation outcomes, not the body-size-to-association-propensity relationship. ✗
That is the correct answer.
